citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
The IACUC has not been reviewing the facility's program for humane care and use of animals at least once every six
months. The reviews were conducted on March 5, 2025, October 22, 2025, and May 7, 2026. Regular reviews are
necessary to ensure IACUC oversight of the facility's program for humane care and use of animals. The IACUC shall
review the facility's program for humane care and use of animals at least once every six months. Correct by June 22,
2026.

citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
The IACUC has not been inspecting the facility's animal facilities at least once every six months. The last inspections
were conducted on March 5, 2025, October 22, 2025, and May 7, 2026. Regular inspections are necessary to ensure
IACUC oversight of the animal facilities. The IACUC shall inspect all of the animal facilities at least once every six months.
Correct by June 22, 2026.

citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
The last letter to the IO was sent over six months ago on October 22, 2025. In order to apprise the Institutional Official of
the status of the facility's programs and facilities and to allow the IO to follow up on correction of significant deficiencies,
the IACUC must complete a report of its evaluations and submit it to the IO in a timely fashion. The IACUC shall prepare
reports of its program for humane care and use of animals and facility inspection at least once every six months and
submit them in a timely manner to the Institutional Official. Correct from this day forward on May 13, 2026.

citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
Protocol EHES, GHES, RHES, CES, CFDP, FES, RC, RE, RF, and CFSP are currently being used for veterinary
technician training with regulated animals and have not been reviewed and approved by the IACUC in over 3 years.
According to the attending veterinarian all protocols in use expired in December 2025. The complete review of activities
(protocols) is important to maintain compliance and to allow the IACUC to ensure that the animal activities meet the AWA
regulations. The IACUC shall conduct complete reviews of activities covered at appropriate intervals as determined by the
IACUC, but not less than every 3 years. Correct by July 6, 2026 and prior to using any animals in future training labs.

citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
Multiple teaching protocols to include EHES, GHES, RHES, CES, CFDP, FES, RC, RE, RF, and CFSP are missing
several required elements. They do not include the total number of animals to be used, a rationale for that number, or
complete descriptions of the procedures. The protocols also fail to identify the total number of labs to be conducted and a
justification for the selected species. Incomplete descriptions of procedures do not allow the IACUC to fully understand
and review the proposed activities involving animal use. This oversight is crucial to monitoring and enforcing animal
welfare standards in research. All protocols submitted to the IACUC for review must contain all five components listed in
this subsection, including the species and the approximate number of animals to be used, a rationale for the species and
number of animals, a complete description of the proposed use of the animals, a description of procedures designed to
assure that discomfort and pain to the animals will be limited to that which is unavoidable for the conduct of scientifically
valuable research, and a description of any euthanasia method to be used.
This inspection was conducted with a facility representative and the exit interview was conducted with the attending
veterinarian.
n

2.31(c)(3) 
   
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). 
The last letter to the IO was sent over six months ago on October 22, 2025. In order to apprise the Institutional Official of

2.31(d)(5) 
   
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). 
Protocol EHES, GHES, RHES, CES, CFDP, FES, RC, RE, RF, and CFSP are currently being used for veterinary 
technician training with regulated animals and have not been reviewed and approved by the IACUC in over 3 years. 
According to the attending veterinarian all protocols in use expired in December 2025. The complete review of activities

(protocols) is important to maintain compliance and to allow the IACUC to ensure that the animal activities meet the AWA 
regulations. The IACUC shall conduct complete reviews of activities covered at appropriate intervals as determined by the 
IACUC, but not less than every 3 years. Correct by July 6, 2026 and prior to using any animals in future training labs. 
2.31(e)  Repeat
   
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). 
Multiple teaching protocols to include EHES, GHES, RHES, CES, CFDP, FES, RC, RE, RF, and CFSP are missing 
several required elements. They do not include the total number of animals to be used, a rationale for that number, or 
complete descriptions of the procedures. The protocols also fail to identify the total number of labs to be conducted and a 
justification for the selected species. Incomplete descriptions of procedures do not allow the IACUC to fully understand 
and review the proposed activities involving animal use. This oversight is crucial to monitoring and enforcing animal 
welfare standards in research. All protocols submitted to the IACUC for review must contain all five components listed in 
this subsection, including the species and the approximate number of animals to be used, a rationale for the species and 
number of animals, a complete description of the proposed use of the animals, a description of procedures designed to 
assure that discomfort and pain to the animals will be limited to that which is unavoidable for the conduct of scientifically 
valuable research, and a description of any euthanasia method to be used. 
 
This inspection was conducted with a facility representative and the exit interview was conducted with the attending 
veterinarian.
